4 min read
Just now

--

Part 4 of the “Concurrency & Parallelism Explained” series. One thread. Thousands of simultaneous connections. No locks. How?
A Node.js server handles 10,000 concurrent connections on a single thread. A Python asyncio app juggles thousands of API calls at once without spawning thousands of threads. To anyone who just learned that concurrency means threads and locks, this sounds impossible — or like magic. It's neither. It's an event loop, and once you see how it works, async programming stops being a mystery you cargo-cult and becomes a model you actually understand.
The whole trick rests on one observation: most programs spend the vast majority of their time waiting, not computing. The event loop is a way to do something useful with all that waiting. Let me show you the machinery.
Threads achieve concurrency by running many things at once. An event loop achieves concurrency by running one thing at a time but never sitting idle — the instant a task starts waiting, it switches to one that’s ready.
The Core Insight: Most Time Is Spent Waiting
Think about what a typical web request does:
receive request (microseconds of CPU)
query the database (10ms — WAITING, CPU idle)
call another API (50ms — WAITING, CPU idle)
format the response (microseconds of CPU)That request spends 60 milliseconds waiting and almost no time computing. With threads, you’d block a whole thread during those waits. The event loop’s insight: while this request waits for the database, the CPU could be starting or finishing other requests. Don’t waste the wait.
How the Event Loop Works
An event loop is a single thread running a simple cycle: pick a ready task, run it until it hits an await (a point where it must wait for I/O), then set it aside and pick another ready task. When the I/O completes, that task becomes ready again:
loop:
- grab a task that's ready to run
- run it until it `await`s something (I/O) or finishes
- when it awaits → park it, remember to resume when the I/O is done
- meanwhile, run OTHER ready tasks
- when an I/O completes → mark its task ready againasync def handle(request):
data = await db.query(...) # at `await`, control returns to the loop;
result = await api.call(data) # other tasks run during these waits
return format(result)Every await is a yield point: "I'm waiting on I/O — loop, go do something else, wake me when it's ready." One thread, thousands of in-flight tasks, all overlapping their waits. That's how a single thread serves 10,000 connections: it's never executing 10,000 things, but it's waiting on 10,000 things while executing whichever handful are ready.
Why No Locks Are Needed
Here’s the elegant part: because the event loop runs on one thread and only switches tasks at explicit await points, your code between awaits runs without interruption. No other task can touch your variables mid-operation, because no other task is running:
# This is SAFE without a lock in async code:
counter += 1 # runs to completion; no await inside, so nothing interrupts itThe race conditions from Part 2 came from threads being interrupted anywhere. In async, you’re only ever interrupted where you wrote await. So you get concurrency without the locking nightmare — a major reason async is popular for I/O-heavy servers. (The catch: you must be careful around await points, where state can change underneath you.)
Fix #1: Use Async for I/O, Never for CPU
The event loop only wins when tasks yield by waiting on I/O. A CPU-bound task never hits an await — it just computes — so it blocks the entire loop, freezing every other task:
# DISASTER in async: this blocks the loop; all 10,000 connections freeze
async def crunch(data):
return sum(x * x for x in data) # pure CPU, no await — hogs the thread# Right: offload CPU work so the loop stays free
result = await loop.run_in_executor(None, crunch, data) # runs in a thread/process poolThis is the same lesson as the Python performance series: async is for waiting, not computing. One blocking CPU task (or a careless blocking library call) stalls everything. Keep the loop free.
Fix #2: Don’t Mix Blocking Calls Into Async Code
A subtle killer: calling a synchronous, blocking function inside async code blocks the whole loop just like CPU work does:
async def handler():
data = requests.get(url) # BLOCKING — freezes the loop!
data = await aiohttp_get(url) # async version — yields properlyUse async-native libraries for I/O (aiohttp not requests, async DB drivers), or push unavoidable blocking calls to a thread (asyncio.to_thread). One blocking call hidden in an async handler can tank your whole server's concurrency.
Putting It Together

• The insight: programs mostly wait; the loop fills the waiting with other work.

• The mechanism: one thread, switch tasks at await, resume when I/O completes.

• No locks needed — single thread, interruption only at explicit await points.

• I/O only — CPU work or blocking calls freeze the loop; offload them.
The Takeaway
The async event loop looks like magic until you see the simple idea underneath: since programs spend most of their time waiting on I/O, a single thread can stay busy by switching to another ready task every time the current one starts to wait. That gives you massive I/O concurrency without the threads-and-locks complexity — and because the one thread only switches at explicit await points, you even escape most race conditions for free. The price is a strict rule: the loop must never be blocked, so async is exclusively for I/O-bound work, never for CPU crunching or hidden blocking calls. Understand the loop, keep it free, and "one thread serving ten thousand connections" stops being mysterious and starts being a tool you wield deliberately.

This is Part 4 of the Concurrency & Parallelism Explained series. Next up: when parallelism actually makes things faster — and the law that says it often won’t as much as you hope.
What clicked for you when async finally made sense? 👇
